content.text: What it is

UAT defect triage and prioritisation for agentic AI features is the structured process by which defects found during user acceptance testing are classified by severity and frequency, assigned a priority level, and routed to the correct team with a target resolution timeline. Unlike functional software defects — which are typically binary (the feature works or it does not) — AI defects exist on a quality spectrum and often cannot be reproduced exactly, which requires a different triage framework than standard bug tracking processes.

The triage framework for AI defects must answer three questions: how often does this failure occur (frequency), how severely does it affect users or the business when it occurs (impact), and what is its root cause (model behaviour, prompt design, post-processing, infrastructure). The answers to these questions determine the priority level and the remediation owner. A defect that occurs on 10% of inputs and causes a data error (high frequency, high impact) blocks the release; a defect that occurs on 0.1% of inputs and produces slightly awkward phrasing (low frequency, low impact) belongs in the product backlog.
Why it matters

Without a structured triage process, AI defects found in UAT produce one of two failure modes. Either every defect is treated as blocking — slowing releases to a crawl while engineering investigates statistical outliers — or defects are informally dismissed as 'AI being AI' without a documented decision. The first failure mode creates shipping friction that makes teams avoid rigorous UAT. The second creates liability when a dismissed defect turns out to be more prevalent than estimated and reaches a significant proportion of users in production.
Benefits
Provides objective, repeatable criteria for deciding which defects block the release and which go to the backlog
Documents the reasoning behind triage decisions for audit and retrospective review
Routes defects to the correct owner (prompt engineer, model team, infrastructure) based on root cause classification
Enables trend analysis: if the same defect type recurs across releases, it signals a systemic issue requiring architectural attention
Creates a manageable defect queue that product managers can prioritise against feature work
Trade-offs and limitations
AI defect frequency is hard to estimate from a UAT sample: a defect seen once in 20 scenarios may occur 1% or 10% of the time in production
Root cause determination requires AI systems knowledge that UAT evaluators may not have — defects need engineering review before root cause is confirmed
Some AI defects are inherent to the model's capabilities and cannot be fixed — the triage process must handle 'accepted risk' as a legitimate disposition
The same defect may appear as both a functional and a quality failure depending on the evaluator's perspective, requiring calibration on classification
Triage decisions made under time pressure before a release deadline are more likely to be biased toward underclassifying severity
How it works

The AI defect triage matrix classifies defects on two axes: impact (the severity of the failure's business or user consequence) and frequency (how often the failure occurs across the input distribution). High impact means the failure causes a materially wrong outcome — a factual error, a safety boundary breach, an incorrect action taken by the agent. Low impact means the failure causes a quality issue — awkward phrasing, an unhelpful but not harmful response, a missed opportunity to provide richer context. High frequency means the failure occurs on more than 5% of inputs in the relevant input class. Low frequency means less than 1% of inputs.

The four quadrants of the matrix map to four priority levels. P1 (block release): high impact, high frequency — the most common class of blocking defects. P2 (fix next sprint): high impact, low frequency — must be fixed but not in the current release because the frequency is low enough to be below the production risk threshold. P3 (fix within two sprints): low impact, high frequency — cosmetic issues that affect many users but do not cause material harm. P4 (backlog): low impact, low frequency — acknowledged but deprioritised unless the backlog specifically addresses quality polish.
Architecture

attrs.alt: UAT Defect Triage Priority Matrix: Impact vs Frequency quadrants — P1 Block release, P2 Fix next sprint, P3 Fix 2 sprints, P4 Backlog

content.text: The matrix is applied after the UAT session by the triage team — product manager, engineering lead, and a domain expert. Each defect is plotted on the matrix based on estimated frequency and measured impact. The triage team must agree on frequency and impact classifications; disagreements are resolved by the product manager with final say.
When to use it

Apply the triage framework immediately after each UAT session, before the sign-off decision is made. Allow one business day for the triage session following the UAT session. For each defect identified in UAT, populate a defect record with: the scenario ID, the actual observed output, the expected output, the evaluator's severity assessment, the frequency estimate, the triage priority level, the root cause classification, and the remediation owner. Complete all defect records before the sign-off meeting so gate owners can make informed approval decisions.
How to implement
Define the frequency thresholds for your feature: high (>5% of inputs in the relevant class), medium (1–5%), low (<1%)
Define the impact levels for your domain: high (data error, safety breach, compliance failure), medium (quality below threshold), low (minor quality issue or UX improvement)
Create a defect record template with: scenario ID, observed output, expected output, frequency estimate, impact level, root cause classification, priority level, and remediation owner
Hold a triage session within one business day of the UAT session with product manager, engineering lead, and one domain expert
For each defect, classify frequency and impact, assign priority, and designate the remediation owner

Add P1 defects to the current sprint as blockers; add P2 defects to the top of the next sprint's backlog; add P3 to the backlog with a two-sprint target; add P4 to the product backlog with no due date
Document all P2+ triage decisions with reasoning so they can be reviewed in retrospectives
Examples

# UAT Defect Record: UD-2026-047

**Scenario:** SC-Q-003 — Regulatory threshold accuracy
**Observed output:** Agent stated the filing deadline was "within 30 days of the event"
**Expected output:** Correct deadline per 2024 rule change: 15 business days

**Frequency estimate:** HIGH (applies to all queries about the specific regulation type;
estimated 8% of total queries in this category based on production query logs)

**Impact:** HIGH (incorrect deadline could cause user to miss filing, resulting in penalty)

**Root cause classification:** Model knowledge cutoff — training data predates the 2024
rule change. Requires prompt injection of current regulatory text.

**Priority:** P1 — Block release
**Remediation owner:** Prompt Engineering team
**Resolution approach:** Inject current regulatory text into system prompt from compliance
knowledge base; add regulation-specific test cases to eval suite before re-UAT

**Target resolution:** Before current release (2026-07-18)

---

# UAT Defect Record: UD-2026-048

**Scenario:** SC-Q-007 — Response formatting
**Observed output:** Agent used bullet points where evaluator expected a numbered list
  for a sequential process
**Expected output:** Numbered list for sequential steps

**Frequency estimate:** LOW (estimated 1–2% of formatting responses based on
manual review of 50 production responses)

**Impact:** LOW (cosmetic issue; does not affect accuracy or safety)

**Priority:** P3 — Fix within 2 sprints
**Remediation owner:** Prompt Engineering team
**Resolution approach:** Add numbered list instruction to system prompt for sequential
process queries; update formatting eval scenarios

**Target resolution:** Sprint 2026-Q3-04
Real-world example

A legal research AI team ran UAT and found 11 defects across 30 scenarios. Without a triage framework, their initial instinct was to treat all 11 as blocking. After applying the frequency-impact matrix, the triage session reclassified: 2 defects were P1 (the agent occasionally cited non-existent case law — high impact, estimated 3% frequency); 3 were P2 (the agent sometimes stated legislation as current when it had been repealed — high impact, estimated 0.5% frequency); 4 were P3 (formatting and length inconsistencies — low impact, estimated 15% frequency); and 2 were P4 (minor phrasing preference issues). The release was blocked for the P1 defects only, with the P2 fixes scheduled in the next sprint. This released two weeks earlier than if all 11 had been treated as blocking.
Common mistakes
Classifying all defects as P1 to be safe: this makes UAT too expensive and trains teams to avoid thorough testing
Not estimating frequency from available data: a defect seen once in UAT has a very wide confidence interval on its production frequency without additional analysis
Assigning root cause to the model without engineering investigation: many apparent model behaviour defects are actually prompt design issues fixable in hours
Treating 'accepted risk' as an informal disposition: document every accepted risk explicitly with the product manager's name and the reasoning
Not following up on P2 and P3 defects: they should appear in sprint backlog reviews; if they consistently get pushed, there is a prioritisation problem to address
Conducting triage without the domain expert present: engineering and product alone cannot accurately estimate business impact for domain-specific defects
Best practices
Estimate defect frequency using production query logs where available, not just the UAT sample — a single occurrence in 20 scenarios is insufficient for frequency classification
Separate root cause investigation (engineering) from priority classification (product + domain expert): root cause can be determined after priority is set
Add every P1 and P2 defect to the automated eval suite before closing the triage session, so they become regression tests
Review the triage backlog quarterly: P3 defects that remain unfixed after two sprints must be re-evaluated for potential priority upgrade
Track the distribution of defect priorities across releases: if P1 defects are increasing over time, the feature quality trend is worsening
Define 'accepted risk' as a valid triage disposition with explicit stakeholder approval — it is better than leaving defects in limbo
Performance and scalability

A UAT session producing 15 defects requires a 90-minute triage session to classify and assign them all. This is sustainable if triage runs the day after UAT. For teams running UAT monthly or less frequently, this is not a bottleneck. For teams running UAT weekly as part of a continuous delivery cadence, consider a lightweight triage process: only defects from failed scenarios require triage; defects from borderline scenarios are reviewed by product manager asynchronously. Reserve the full triage session for releases with three or more UAT failures.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Jira
Defect tracking and sprint assignment
Supports custom fields for AI defect classification
Linear
Defect tracking (startup-friendly)
Fast triage workflows; good for high-velocity teams
Notion
Defect record documentation
Good for storing the narrative alongside structured fields
Weights and Biases
Eval regression tracking
Links defects to eval dataset updates and model versions
Spreadsheet
Lightweight triage for small teams
Sufficient for teams with under 20 UAT defects per cycle
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Frequency-impact matrix triage (this topic)
Objective; comparable across releases
Requires calibration of frequency estimates
Severity-only classification
Simpler; faster
Misses low-severity high-frequency defects that accumulate
Engineering-only triage
Fast; technical expertise
Business impact assessment requires domain expert
Blocking all defects
Maximum safety
Makes UAT too expensive; teams stop doing thorough testing
Security and reliability

UAT defect records containing actual AI output samples may include confidential business data if the UAT session used real or near-real data. Apply the same data classification controls to defect records as to the UAT session data. Store defect records in a tracked issue management system with access controls matching the data sensitivity. When a defect reveals a safety boundary weakness, treat the defect record itself as a security-sensitive document and restrict access to the triage team and security leads until the defect is fixed.
Root cause classification for AI defects

AI defects require a different root cause taxonomy than traditional software defects. In traditional software, root causes fall into categories like logic error, null pointer, off-by-one, or integration failure. For agentic AI, root causes span five categories: model knowledge limitations (the model lacks current or complete knowledge needed to answer correctly), prompt design failures (the system prompt does not adequately constrain or guide the model's behaviour for the given input class), post-processing gaps (the model produced reasonable output but the post-processing pipeline transformed it incorrectly), retrieval failures (RAG retrieval returned irrelevant or outdated context that misled the model), and inherent model limitations (the capability simply does not exist in the current model generation and cannot be fixed without a different model).

Correctly classifying root causes during triage determines who owns the remediation. Model knowledge limitations route to the data and context pipeline team to update the knowledge base or retrieval index. Prompt design failures route to the prompt engineering team. Post-processing gaps route to the application engineering team. Retrieval failures route to the search and relevance team. Inherent model limitations are classified as accepted risk, documented in the known limitations section of the sign-off checklist, and potentially added to the model selection criteria for the next model upgrade cycle.
Model knowledge: training data gap or cutoff — remediation: update knowledge base or retrieval index
Prompt design: system prompt does not constrain the target behaviour — remediation: prompt engineering sprint
Post-processing: correct model output transformed incorrectly — remediation: application engineering fix
Retrieval: RAG returned irrelevant or outdated context — remediation: search and relevance team tuning
Inherent limitation: capability outside current model generation — remediation: accepted risk; track for next model upgrade
Statistical approaches to frequency estimation

Estimating defect frequency from a UAT sample is inherently imprecise. A defect seen once in 20 scenarios could reflect a true frequency of 1% to 15% in production, depending on how representative the 20 scenarios are. To improve frequency estimates, combine three data sources: the UAT sample observation (raw), production query log analysis (historical), and an adversarial sampling analysis (how easy is it to trigger the defect?).

Production query log analysis is the most reliable frequency estimator for features already deployed to some users. Filter the production query logs for prompts similar to the defect-triggering scenario and estimate the proportion that would produce the defect. For features not yet deployed, use adversarial sampling: ask the prompt engineering team to estimate how easy it is to trigger the defect with common user inputs. A defect that is easy to trigger (requires only the most common phrasings of a normal user request) should be classified as high frequency even if it occurred only once in the UAT sample. A defect that required an unusual, atypical prompt to trigger should be classified as low frequency.
Combine three data sources for frequency estimation: UAT observation, production log analysis, and adversarial sampling difficulty
Production log analysis is the gold standard for features with existing production traffic — use it when available
Adversarial sampling: ask the prompt team to estimate how easy it is to trigger the defect with common user phrasings
Easy-to-trigger defects should be classified as high frequency even with a single UAT observation
Document the frequency estimation methodology in the defect record so the estimate can be contested and revised with new data
Communicating defect status to stakeholders

UAT defect triage results must be communicated clearly to multiple stakeholders: engineering needs precise technical details to implement fixes; product management needs priority and timeline; business stakeholders need a simplified view of what risks are being carried and what will be fixed before launch. Prepare a tiered defect report with three layers: an executive summary (how many defects total, how many blocking, overall release recommendation), a product summary (defect list with priority, owner, and timeline), and a technical detail view (full defect records for engineering teams).

For release go/no-go meetings, present the executive summary with a visual traffic light: green (no P1 defects), amber (P1 defects present but remediation in progress), red (P1 defects not yet triaged or unresolvable before the release window). The traffic light makes the release decision visible at a glance without requiring stakeholders to interpret defect counts. Include the date by which P1 remediation must be complete for the planned release date to hold.
Prepare three-layer defect reports: executive summary (decision-level), product summary (owner/timeline), technical detail (engineering reference)
Use traffic light status in go/no-go meetings: green (no P1s), amber (P1s in remediation), red (P1s unresolved)
Include the P1 remediation deadline in the go/no-go meeting agenda so the release date decision is explicit
Never present raw defect counts to executives without context: 'five defects' is meaningless; 'one release blocker, four backlog items' is actionable
Send a daily P1 status update to gate owners during the remediation window so there are no surprises on release day
Defect patterns and systemic issues

When the same type of defect appears across multiple UAT sessions or multiple features, it signals a systemic issue rather than an isolated incident. Track defect patterns in a shared defect log that spans all features and all UAT sessions. If factual accuracy defects consistently appear in Q1 and Q2 but not Q3 and Q4, this may correlate with a model knowledge cutoff pattern. If formatting defects spike after a particular system prompt template was adopted, the template is likely the root cause. Reviewing defect patterns quarterly allows the team to identify and address systemic issues — a problematic system prompt template, a retrieval index that is not being kept current, a post-processing step that systematically truncates long responses — that produce repeated defects across features.

When a systemic issue is identified, the remediation is cross-cutting rather than feature-specific. A knowledge base indexing gap that affects five features requires a single fix to the indexing pipeline, not five separate prompt engineering changes. Identifying these systemic issues through defect pattern analysis is significantly more efficient than treating each defect in isolation. Assign a systemic issue owner at the platform level who reviews defect patterns monthly and initiates cross-cutting remediation work.
Maintain a shared defect log spanning all features and all UAT sessions for cross-feature pattern analysis
Review defect patterns monthly at the platform level: look for recurring defect types, correlated with prompt versions, model versions, or data updates
When the same defect type appears in three or more features, escalate to a systemic issue with a platform-level owner
Systemic issues require cross-cutting remediation (pipeline fix, template update) rather than feature-level workarounds
Track systemic issue resolution time separately from individual defect resolution time as a platform quality metric
Key takeaways
Classify AI UAT defects on two axes: frequency (how often) and impact (how severe), not just severity alone
The four priority levels map to concrete actions: P1 blocks release, P2 fixes next sprint, P3 fixes within two sprints, P4 goes to backlog
Use production query logs to calibrate frequency estimates — UAT sample sizes are too small for reliable frequency classification alone
Add every P1 and P2 defect to the automated eval suite before closing triage, making them regression tests
Document 'accepted risk' explicitly with stakeholder approval — informal dismissal of defects creates liability
Review the triage backlog quarterly to prevent P3 defects from becoming P1 problems through neglect
